tier_2_handbook.definition: Ampullary adenocarcinoma is defined as a malignant tumor originating from the ampulla of Vater, an anatomically complex region formed by three components: the ampulla itself, the intraduodenal portion of the bile duct, and the intraduodenal portion of the pancreatic duct [1, MS-2]. The ampulla of Vater is comprised of two mucosal tissue types: pancreatobiliary ductal mucosa and intestinal mucosa, which gives rise to two distinct histologic subtypes of ampullary cancer [MS-2]. Ampullary cancers must be distinguished from periampullary cancers, which may arise from locations encompassing the head of the pancreas, distal bile duct, duodenum, or ampulla of Vater [1, MS-2]. Distinction of periampullary tumors based on site of origin is particularly challenging, especially for large tumors that have invaded surrounding organs at presentation [16, 17, MS-2]. Each ampullary cancer subtype seems to resemble its periampullary counterpart in terms of biological behavior and prognosis, with the pancreatobiliary subtype demonstrating higher lymph node involvement and worse survival than the intestinal subtype [13, 18, 20, 24-26, MS-2]. Ampullary tumors generally have a more favorable outcome when compared to other periampullary malignancies [4, 6, 7, 10-15, MS-2]. In a single-institutional review of 2564 resected periampullary adenocarcinomas, the median survival for ampullary cancer was 47 months compared to 19, 23, and 54 months for pancreatic, biliary, and duodenal cancer, respectively [10, MS-2].

subtypes.name: Intestinal type
subtypes.description: Characterized by the presence of large tubules lined by tall columnar cells with elongated, pseudostratified, hyperchromatic nuclei resembling colonic-type adenocarcinoma. Immunophenotypic staining profile is typically positive for CK20, CDX2, and/or MUC2 with negative MUC1, or positive for CK20, CDX2, and MUC2 irrespective of MUC1 staining.
subtypes.frequency: Proportion varies widely between study populations
key_associations: In a retrospective study of 95 ampullary cancers, OS was 98 months for intestinal subtype vs. 25 months for pancreatobiliary subtype (P < .001) [25]
Genomic signature similar to colorectal adenocarcinoma
5-year OS for node-negative intestinal subtype was 83.7% vs. 33.2% (P = .015) [122]

subtypes.name: Pancreatobiliary type
subtypes.description: Characterized by variably differentiated glands lined by non-stratified cuboidal or low columnar eosinophilic epithelium exhibiting round to oval, irregular, hypochromatic, or hyperchromatic nuclei with vesicular chromatin and irregular nuclear contours and a high nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio. Abundant desmoplastic stroma may be present. Immunophenotypic staining profile is positive for MUC1 and negative for CDX2 and MUC2 irrespective of CK20 staining.
subtypes.frequency: Proportion varies widely between study populations
key_associations: In a retrospective study, OS was 25 months for pancreatobiliary subtype vs. 14 months for pancreatic cancer (P = .123) [25]
Genomic signature similar to pancreatic adenocarcinoma
High incidence of KRAS mutations (~30%-40%) [32, 33, 36, MS-4]
Higher rate of early recurrence (71.4% vs. 28.6% within 6 months of surgery, P = .001) [84]
OS after pancreatoduodenectomy: 118 months for pancreatobiliary/mixed vs. 156 months for intestinal (P = .003) [87]
Adjuvant therapy beneficial (improved median OS 85 vs. 65 months for observation, P = .005) [87]

subtypes.name: Mixed type
subtypes.description: A significant proportion of ampullary adenocarcinomas may be of mixed or ambiguous phenotype. These ambiguous cases should be classified as tubular adenocarcinoma with mixed features, with the predominant pattern noted in the pathology report for data collection purposes and future analysis.
key_associations: Genomic heterogeneity within each histologic subtype [43]
There is overlap in immunohistochemical profiles of intestinal and pancreatobiliary-type adenocarcinomas [8, 17, 18]
Current data on independent prognostic and predictive value are conflicting [11-16]

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The genomic landscape of ampullary cancer shows important similarities and differences with other periampullary cancers. KRAS mutations occur in approximately 30%-40% of ampullary cancers, which is comparable to duodenal cancer but much lower than pancreatic cancer (~90%) [32-36, MS-4]. KRAS mutations appear more frequently in pancreatobiliary subtypes, though the distribution across histologic subtypes remains unclear due to small patient numbers [32, 33, MS-4]. Other somatic alterations reported include mutations in APC, TP53, CDKN2A, DPC4, ELF3, PIK3CA, and SMAD4, as well as HER2 amplifications and microsatellite instability (MSI) [37-41, MS-4]. Pathogenic germline mutations include BRCA1/2, ATM, RAD50, and MUTYH [39, 42, MS-4]. A genomic classification study of 3411 patients with periampullary cancers demonstrated high concordance between histologic subtypes and their respective genomic categories: the pancreatobiliary subtype has genomic signature similar to pancreatic adenocarcinoma (characterized by high KRAS mutations), while the intestinal subtype has genomic signature similar to colorectal adenocarcinoma (characterized by APC and PIK3CA mutations, higher TMB, and dMMR) [43, MS-4]. Somatic molecular profiling for metastatic disease should identify fusions (ALK, NRG1, NTRK, ROS1, FGFR2, and RET), mutations (BRAF, BRCA1/2, KRAS, and PALB2), amplifications (HER2), MSI, dMMR, or TMB using comprehensive genomic profiling via FDA-approved and/or validated NGS-based assays [h]. RNA sequencing assays are preferred for detecting RNA fusions because gene fusions are better detected by RNA-based NGS [h]. Testing on tumor tissue is preferred, though cell-free DNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible [h]. HER2 overexpression occurs in 13% of ampullary cancers [MS-15]. BRAF V600E mutations have been reported with response rates of 37.9%-47% to dabrafenib/trametinib [158, 159].

epidemiology.incidence: Ampullary adenocarcinoma accounts for only 0.2% of gastrointestinal malignancies and 6% of all periampullary cancers [2, MS-2]. The condition is relatively rare but important given pathologic variations and associated prognosis [MS-2].
epidemiology.mortality: The 5-year overall survival (OS) for ampullary cancer is between 35% and 50%, varying greatly based on patient age, TNM classification, differentiation, grade, and treatment modality received [3-9, MS-2]. The 5-year OS for AJCC 7th Edition stage I is 64%, stage II is 27%, and stage III + IV is 17% [7, MS-2].
epidemiology.trends: A large population-based study of 1301 patients who underwent resection for ampullary cancer reported significantly higher 5- and 10-year disease-specific survival (DSS) for node-negative versus node-positive disease (59.4% vs. 28.4%; P < .001 and 54.1% vs. 21.9%; P < .001, respectively) [79]. In a single-institutional review, median survival for ampullary cancer was 47 months compared to 19, 23, and 54 months for pancreatic, biliary, and duodenal cancer, respectively [10, MS-2].
epidemiology.demographics: No specific SEER/GLOBOCAN demographic breakdown by race, ethnicity, or sex is provided in this guideline document. The guideline notes that most studies do not report how sex and gender data are collected and use these terms interchangeably or inconsistently [MS-3].

risk_factors.detail: Genetic testing for inherited mutations is recommended for any patient with confirmed ampullary adenocarcinoma or positive family history of cancer. Pathogenic mutations include ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CDKN2A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2, STK11, and TP53 [b].
Ampullary adenomas can arise sporadically or in the setting of hereditary polyposis syndromes such as familial adenomatous polyposis [MS-5].

risk_factors.factor: Pathogenic germline variants (BRCA1/2, ATM, RAD50, MUTYH)
risk_factors.detail: Pathogenic germline mutations reported in ampullary cancer include BRCA1/2, ATM, RAD50, and MUTYH [39, 42, MS-4].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Ampullary adenocarcinoma often presents with symptoms of biliary obstruction, including jaundice, which is the most common initial sign due to the tumor's location at the ampulla of Vater, which obstructs the bile duct. Patients may also experience abdominal pain, typically in the epigastric or right upper quadrant, weight loss, and pruritus. Diagnosis is frequently made incidentally during endoscopic or radiographic evaluation for other gastrointestinal complaints or through workup of obstructive jaundice. The tumor's complex anatomical origin from the ampulla of Vater can make clinical distinction from other periampullary cancers challenging, particularly for large, invasive tumors.

symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.detail: Painless, progressive obstructive jaundice is the most common presenting symptom, resulting from bile duct obstruction by the tumor.

symptoms.name: Abdominal Pain
symptoms.detail: Epigastric or right upper quadrant pain, which may be vague and intermittent.

symptoms.name: Weight Loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss, often associated with anorexia and early satiety.

symptoms.name: Pruritus
symptoms.detail: Generalized itching due to bile salt deposition in the skin.

symptoms.name: Nausea and Vomiting
symptoms.detail: May occur due to gastric outlet obstruction or duodenal involvement.

signs.name: Jaundice
signs.detail: Yellowing of the sclera and skin.

signs.name: Palpable Gallbladder (Courvoisier's Sign)
signs.detail: A palpable, non-tender gallbladder in the setting of painless jaundice.

signs.name: Hepatomegaly
signs.detail: Enlargement of the liver, which may be due to biliary obstruction or metastatic disease.

atypical_or_missed: Tumors may be asymptomatic and found incidentally on imaging or endoscopy performed for other indications.
Endoscopic biopsies of ampullary adenocarcinoma have high false-negative rates (approximately 20% to 40%), which can delay diagnosis.
Adenocarcinoma arising within an ampullary adenoma can be missed on initial endoscopic biopsies.
Large tumors that have invaded surrounding organs may mimic pancreatic or duodenal cancers, complicating the identification of the primary site.

red_flags: Unexplained weight loss greater than 5-10% of body weight
Epigastric or right upper quadrant pain persisting or worsening
Cholangitis or fever associated with biliary obstruction
Signs of gastrointestinal bleeding (e.g., melena, hematemesis)

diagnostic.rationale: EUS is recommended as an adjunct to EGD to assess the depth of invasion, evaluate for pancreatic invasion (which mandates pancreatoduodenectomy), and obtain tissue for histologic diagnosis and subtyping.
diagnostic.key_findings: Can determine the extent of tumor invasion, assess regional lymph nodes, and guide biopsy. EUS is noted as more specific and sensitive than CT in some studies.

diagnostic.rationale: Provides direct visualization of the ampulla, allows for biopsy, and can assess for duodenal invasion or synchronous lesions.
diagnostic.key_findings: Direct visualization of the tumor, mucosal changes, and potential bleeding sites.

diagnostic.test: Core biopsy of the primary or metastatic site
diagnostic.rationale: Core biopsy is recommended, if possible, to obtain adequate tissue for molecular profiling or other ancillary studies.
diagnostic.key_findings: Provides sufficient tissue for histologic diagnosis, subtyping, and molecular testing.

diagnostic.rationale: To exclude synchronous colonic polyps or neoplasms, especially in patients with hereditary syndromes like familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP).
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of synchronous adenomas or carcinomas.

staging.test: Multi-detector computed tomography (MDCT) pancreatic protocol of abdomen and pelvis with contrast
staging.rationale: Preferred imaging tool for dedicated pancreatic imaging. Provides assessment of tumor extent, vascular involvement, lymphadenopathy, and distant metastases.
staging.indication: All patients with suspected or confirmed ampullary adenocarcinoma for initial staging and assessment of resectability.

staging.test: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) with and without contrast
staging.rationale: Most commonly used as a problem-solving tool, particularly for characterization of CT-indeterminate liver lesions or when contrast-enhanced CT cannot be obtained (e.g., severe iodinated contrast allergy).
staging.indication: Patients with indeterminate liver lesions on CT, contraindication to CT contrast, or need for additional characterization of biliary/pancreatic ductal anatomy (MRCP).

staging.test: Positron Emission Tomography/Computed Tomography (PET/CT) without iodinated IV contrast
staging.rationale: The role remains unclear but can be used per institutional preference, especially if MRI cannot be performed (e.g., pacemaker-dependent patient). It is not a substitute for high-quality, contrast-enhanced CT.
staging.indication: Consider in selected cases to evaluate for distant metastases or equivocal findings on other imaging.

staging.rationale: To rule out peritoneal or liver metastases not detected on preoperative imaging prior to surgery or chemoradiation.
staging.indication: Consider prior to surgery or neoadjuvant therapy, especially in high-risk cases.

biomarkers.rationale: Tumor marker for ampullary adenocarcinoma, used for baseline measurement and monitoring response to therapy.
biomarkers.when: Baseline at diagnosis and periodically during surveillance.

biomarkers.rationale: Tumor marker used in conjunction with CA 19-9 for baseline assessment and monitoring.
biomarkers.when: Baseline at diagnosis and periodically during surveillance.

biomarkers.test: Tumor/somatic molecular profiling, preferably using a next-generation sequencing (NGS) assay
biomarkers.rationale: Recommended for patients with metastatic disease who are candidates for anti-cancer therapy to identify clinically actionable and/or emerging alterations.
biomarkers.when: For patients with metastatic disease who are candidates for anti-cancer therapy.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended if clinically indicated, not mandatory for all patients.

biomarkers.test: Her2 overexpression via immunohistochemistry (IHC) ± fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH)
biomarkers.rationale: To identify HER2 amplification, which is a targetable alteration in ampullary adenocarcinoma.
biomarkers.when: As part of molecular profiling for metastatic disease.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended as part of comprehensive molecular profiling.

biomarkers.test: Microsatellite instability (MSI) or mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR)
biomarkers.rationale: To identify patients who may benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
biomarkers.when: As part of molecular profiling for metastatic disease.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended as part of comprehensive molecular profiling.

biomarkers.rationale: To identify patients with high TMB (≥10 mut/Mb) who may benefit from pembrolizumab.
biomarkers.when: As part of molecular profiling for metastatic disease.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended as part of comprehensive molecular profiling.

biomarkers.test: Genetic testing for inherited mutations (e.g., ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CDKN2A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2, STK11, TP53)
biomarkers.rationale: Recommended for any patient with confirmed ampullary adenocarcinoma or positive family history of cancer, using comprehensive gene panels for hereditary cancer syndromes.

do_not_miss: Core biopsy for molecular profiling if possible (preferable to fine-needle aspiration).
Genetic testing for inherited mutations, especially in patients with a family history of cancer or young age at diagnosis.
Assessment of histologic subtype (pancreatobiliary vs. intestinal) as it may influence prognosis and treatment.
Evaluation of resectability status via high-quality, dedicated pancreatic imaging (MDCT or MRI).
Multidisciplinary consultation at a high-volume center.
avoid: Routine use of PET/CT as a substitute for high-quality, contrast-enhanced CT or MRI for staging.
Stent placement prior to planned surgery unless indicated for symptoms of cholangitis, severe jaundice, or if surgery is delayed.
Relying solely on endoscopic biopsies for definitive diagnosis due to high false-negative rates; consider repeat biopsy or alternative sampling methods.
Overuse of ERCP/PTC for purely diagnostic purposes when non-invasive imaging is adequate; these carry procedural risks.

staging.system: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition (2017)

t_stages.description: Tumor limited to ampulla of Vater or sphincter of Oddi or tumor invades beyond the sphincter of Oddi (perisphincteric invasion) and/or into the duodenal submucosa.
Tumor limited to ampulla of Vater or sphincter of Oddi.
Tumor invades beyond the sphincter of Oddi (perisphincteric invasion) and/or into the duodenal submucosa.
Tumor invades into the muscularis propria of the duodenum.

Tumor directly invades the pancreas (up to 0.5 cm) or tumor extends more than 0.5 cm into the pancreas, or extends into peripancreatic or periduodenal tissue or duodenal serosa without involvement of the celiac axis or superior mesenteric artery.
Tumor directly invades pancreas (up to 0.5 cm).
Tumor extends more than 0.5 cm into the pancreas, or extends into peripancreatic tissue or periduodenal tissue or duodenal serosa without involvement of the celiac axis or superior mesenteric artery.
Tumor involves the celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and/or common hepatic artery, irrespective of size.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Metastasis to one to three regional lymph nodes.
Metastasis to four or more regional lymph nodes.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ; excellent prognosis with surgical resection.
Tumor limited to the ampulla of Vater or sphincter of Oddi; excellent prognosis with surgery.
Tumor invades beyond the sphincter of Oddi/duodenal submucosa or into the duodenal muscularis propria; good prognosis with surgery.
Tumor directly invades the pancreas (up to 0.5 cm); intermediate prognosis; surgery with consideration of adjuvant therapy.
Tumor extends more than 0.5 cm into the pancreas or into peripancreatic/periduodenal tissue; higher risk of recurrence; adjuvant therapy often recommended.

stage_groups.criteria: T1a, T1b, T2, T3a, T3b, N1, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Regional lymph node metastasis (1-3 nodes); higher risk; adjuvant systemic therapy recommended.

stage_groups.criteria: T4, any N, M0; or any T, N2, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor involves major vascular structures (T4) or extensive lymph node metastasis (4 or more nodes); often considered locally advanced; multidisciplinary management including neoadjuvant therapy may be considered.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative or palliative, depending on resectability

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastatic disease; not curable with surgery; systemic therapy and palliative care.

staging_pearls: The ampulla of Vater is anatomically complex, comprising the ampulla, intraduodenal bile duct, and intraduodenal pancreatic duct. Tumors can arise from any component, making staging challenging.

Adequate lymph node assessment is critical for staging. The panel consensus is to assess at least 12 lymph nodes in pancreatoduodenectomy specimens, though this may be more challenging after neoadjuvant therapy.
The distinction between pancreatobiliary and intestinal histologic subtypes is important for prognosis and may influence therapy, but is not part of the TNM staging system.
AJCC staging for ampullary adenocarcinoma is specific to this site and differs from staging for pancreatic or biliary cancers.

Use of a standardized radiology reporting template (e.g., the Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma Radiology Reporting Template) is recommended to ensure complete assessment of vascular involvement and resectability.

management_principles.overview: The management of ampullary adenocarcinoma is rooted in a multidisciplinary, stage-adapted approach that integrates local and systemic therapies with curative intent for localized disease and palliative intent for metastatic or recurrent disease. Treatment philosophy emphasizes early detection, accurate histologic subtyping (pancreatobiliary vs. intestinal), and molecular profiling to guide therapy selection. The ampulla of Vater is an anatomically complex region, and tumors here often present with early symptoms like jaundice, which may allow for earlier intervention compared to other periampullary cancers. Prognosis varies significantly based on TNM stage, differentiation grade, lymph node involvement, resection margin status, and histologic subtype, with the intestinal subtype generally having a more favorable prognosis than the pancreatobiliary subtype. A multimodal strategy is employed, with surgery (pancreatoduodenectomy) serving as the cornerstone for resectable disease, while systemic therapy is utilized in the neoadjuvant, adjuvant, and metastatic settings. Radiation therapy plays a complementary role in select scenarios to enhance local control. Supportive care and palliation are integral throughout the disease continuum to manage symptoms such as biliary obstruction, pain, and nutritional deficiencies, thereby maintaining quality of life [MS-2, MS-6, AMP-G].

by_intent.approach: Primary treatment is surgical resection (pancreatoduodenectomy). Neoadjuvant systemic therapy with or without chemoradiation may be considered for high-risk features, including equivocal imaging, markedly elevated tumor markers, large tumors, significant lymphadenopathy, or poor nutritional status. Adjuvant therapy is recommended postoperatively based on pathologic stage [AMP-4, AMP-5, MS-6, MS-9].

Adjuvant systemic therapy is initiated within 12 weeks of surgery if medically fit. The regimen depends on histologic subtype and stage. For stage I, adjuvant chemotherapy or observation may be considered; for stages II and III, chemotherapy is recommended, often with subsequent chemoradiation for higher-risk features. Total duration of systemic therapy is typically 6 months [AMP-5, MS-9, MS-10].

by_intent.patient_group: Metastatic or locally advanced unresectable disease
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy is the mainstay, with regimens tailored to performance status and histologic subtype. Local therapies like radiation or surgery for oligometastatic disease may be considered for symptom control or in select cases with response to systemic therapy. Best supportive care is emphasized for poor performance status [AMP-6, MS-12].

Options include clinical trials, alternative systemic regimens, targeted therapy based on molecular profiling, or palliative radiation for symptom relief. Treatment selection is guided by prior therapies, performance status, and molecular markers [AMP-7, MS-14].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary consultation is essential for optimal management. The team should include expertise from diagnostic imaging, interventional endoscopy, medical oncology, radiation oncology, surgery, pathology, geriatric medicine, genetic counseling, and palliative care. Consultation with a registered dietitian is also recommended to address nutritional needs. Decisions about diagnostic management, resectability, and treatment planning should be made at high-volume centers with experience in periampullary cancers [AMP-3, MS-6].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is a critical determinant of treatment eligibility and intensity. Good PS is defined as ECOG 0–1 with good biliary drainage and adequate nutritional intake. Intermediate PS is defined as ECOG 2, where dose or schedule adjustments may be considered. Poor PS is defined as ECOG 3, for which palliative and best supportive care is preferred, though systemic or targeted therapy may be considered in selected cases [AMP-6, AMP-7, MS-12].

nodes.criteria: Benign ampullary adenoma confirmed, without suspicion of adenocarcinoma

options.label: Preferred if technically feasible at high-volume center
options.recommendation: Endoscopic ampullectomy is recommended for adenomas without suspicion of adenocarcinoma, including those with high-grade dysplasia [AMP-2, MS-5].

options.label: If endoscopic removal not possible
options.recommendation: Can be chosen at surgical centers with expertise [AMP-2].

options.label: If endoscopic removal not possible or for deeper invasion
options.recommendation: Pancreatoduodenectomy is indicated if endoscopic removal is not possible or if there is suspicion of adenocarcinoma [AMP-2, MS-5].

options.label: Endoscopic or surgical
options.recommendation: Re-excision can be considered [AMP-2].

options.label: If re-excision not feasible or for persistent positive margins
options.recommendation: Pancreatoduodenectomy is recommended [AMP-2].

nodes.criteria: Adenocarcinoma found on pathology after resection of adenoma

options.label: Proceed to adenocarcinoma pathways
options.recommendation: Follow guidelines for ampullary adenocarcinoma based on stage [AMP-2, MS-5].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy may be indicated based on pathologic staging of adenocarcinoma.

management_pathways.title: Localized Ampullary Adenocarcinoma without High-Risk Features

nodes.criteria: Localized disease, no metastases, resectable, good performance status

options.label: Pancreatoduodenectomy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Pancreatoduodenectomy is the standard surgical approach for localized disease [AMP-4, MS-6].

options.label: Postoperative based on stage
options.recommendation: Adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended within 12 weeks of surgery. For stage I, adjuvant chemotherapy or observation; for stages II and III, adjuvant chemotherapy ± chemoradiation [AMP-5, MS-9].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is based on final pathologic stage and histologic subtype.

nodes.criteria: Localized disease, resectable, but with high-risk features (equivocal imaging, elevated markers, large tumors, etc.)

options.label: Consider systemic therapy with or without chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy may be considered, with regimens varying by histologic subtype. Participation in a clinical trial is encouraged [AMP-4, MS-6, MS-9].

options.label: Pancreatoduodenectomy after neoadjuvant therapy if resectable
options.recommendation: Surgery is performed after neoadjuvant therapy and restaging [AMP-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Post-surgical adjuvant therapy depends on response to neoadjuvant therapy and final pathology.

options.label: First-line based on subtype and PS
options.recommendation: Treated similarly to metastatic disease with systemic therapy. Chemoradiation may be considered for local control [AMP-4, MS-12].

options.label: For second opinion on unresectability
options.recommendation: Refer to tertiary cancer center for assessment [AMP-4].

management_pathways.title: Postoperative Adjuvant Treatment for Resected Ampullary Adenocarcinoma

options.label: Systemic therapy or observation
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is recommended, or observation may be considered [AMP-5, MS-9].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Initiation within 12 weeks of surgery if fit.

options.label: Systemic therapy ± subsequent chemoradiation or observation
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is recommended, with consideration of subsequent chemoradiation for high-risk features [AMP-5, MS-9].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Total duration of systemic therapy typically 6 months.

options.label: Systemic therapy ± subsequent chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is recommended, with chemoradiation often considered for node-positive or margin-positive disease [AMP-5, MS-9, MS-11].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Chemotherapy given prior to chemoradiation if positive margins.

management_pathways.title: First-Line Treatment for Metastatic Ampullary Adenocarcinoma

nodes.criteria: Metastatic disease, good/intermediate PS (ECOG 0-2), pancreatobiliary/mixed type

options.label: First-line chemotherapy regimens
options.recommendation: Options include albumin-bound paclitaxel/gemcitabine, capecitabine/gemcitabine, cisplatin/gemcitabine, cisplatin/gemcitabine + durvalumab, FOLFIRINOX or modified FOLFIRINOX, FOLFOX, or NALIRIFOX [AMP-6, MS-12, MS-13].

options.label: Based on molecular profiling
options.recommendation: If actionable alterations: entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib for NTRK fusion; ipilimumab + nivolumab for MSI-H/dMMR (intestinal type only); pembrolizumab for MSI-H, dMMR, or TMB-H; selpercatinib for RET fusion; dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E mutation [AMP-6, MS-13].

nodes.criteria: Metastatic disease, good/intermediate PS (ECOG 0-2), intestinal type

options.label: First-line chemotherapy regimens
options.recommendation: Options include CAPEOX, CAPEOX + bevacizumab, FOLFIRI, FOLFIRI + bevacizumab, FOLFIRINOX, FOLFIRINOX + bevacizumab, FOLFOX, or FOLFOX + bevacizumab [AMP-6, MS-12, MS-13].

options.label: Based on molecular profiling
options.recommendation: Same as for pancreatobiliary/mixed type, except nivolumab monotherapy for MSI-H/dMMR in intestinal type [AMP-6, MS-13].

options.label: Preferred
options.recommendation: Palliative care is preferred; systemic therapy may be considered with caution [AMP-6, MS-12].

options.label: Simplified regimens if fit
options.recommendation: For pancreatobiliary/mixed type: capecitabine, fluorouracil/leucovorin, or gemcitabine. For intestinal type: capecitabine or fluorouracil/leucovorin [AMP-6, MS-12].

options.label: Consider based on molecular profile
options.recommendation: Similar targeted options as for good PS, with nivolumab monotherapy for MSI-H/dMMR intestinal type [AMP-6, MS-13].

management_pathways.title: Treatment for Disease Progression in Ampullary Adenocarcinoma

options.label: Preferred if available
options.recommendation: Enrollment in a clinical trial is strongly encouraged [AMP-7, MS-14].

options.label: Subsequent-line based on prior therapy and subtype
options.recommendation: For pancreatobiliary/mixed type: if prior gemcitabine-based therapy, use fluoropyrimidine-based regimen; if prior fluoropyrimidine-based, use gemcitabine-based regimen. For intestinal type: if prior oxaliplatin-based, use FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab [AMP-7, MS-14, MS-15].

options.label: Based on molecular profiling
options.recommendation: Options include adagrasib or sotorasib for KRAS G12C mutation; cisplatin/gemcitabine for BRCA1/2 mutations; dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF V600E; entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib for NTRK fusion; fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki for HER2 positive; selpercatinib for RET fusion; dostarlimab-gxly, ipilimumab + nivolumab, or pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR/TMB-H if no prior immunotherapy [AMP-7, MS-15].

options.label: For severe pain refractory to analgesics
options.recommendation: Palliative RT may be used for symptom control [AMP-7, MS-14].

options.label: Preferred
options.recommendation: Best supportive care is preferred; consider systemic or targeted therapy if clinically indicated [AMP-7, MS-14].

options.label: Simplified regimens if considered
options.recommendation: For pancreatobiliary/mixed type: capecitabine or fluorouracil/leucovorin if prior gemcitabine-based; gemcitabine if prior fluoropyrimidine-based. For intestinal type: capecitabine or fluorouracil/leucovorin [AMP-7, MS-15].

options.label: Consider based on molecular profile
options.recommendation: Similar targeted options as for good PS, with dostarlimab-gxly, ipilimumab + nivolumab, nivolumab, or pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR/TMB-H [AMP-7, MS-15].

options.label: For symptom control
options.recommendation: Palliative RT may be used [AMP-7, MS-14].

items.text: Dedicated pancreatic protocol CT of abdomen with contrast (preferred) or MRI with and without contrast for staging.
CT chest and pelvic CT or MRI as part of complete staging.
Consider MRI for indeterminate liver lesions as clinically indicated.
PET/CT role unclear; may be used per institutional preference but not a substitute for contrast-enhanced CT.
Liver function tests, baseline CA 19-9, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA).
Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with side-viewing endoscope ± endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with biopsy for histologic diagnosis and assessment of invasion.
Consider endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) or percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) as clinically indicated for biliary drainage or tissue diagnosis.
Colonoscopy to exclude synchronous colonic polyps or neoplasms if not previously performed.
Core biopsy recommended if possible to obtain adequate tissue for molecular profiling or other ancillary studies.
Histologic subtyping as pancreatobiliary, intestinal, or mixed if possible.

Tumor/somatic molecular profiling preferably using next-generation sequencing (NGS) assay for metastatic disease candidates for anti-cancer therapy to identify actionable alterations (e.g., fusions in ALK, NRG1, NTRK, ROS1, FGFR2, RET; mutations in BRAF, BRCA1/2, KRAS, PALB2; amplifications in HER2; MSI, dMMR, TMB; HER2 overexpression via IHC ± FISH). RNA sequencing preferred for fusions. Testing on tissue preferred; cell-free DNA if tissue not feasible.

Genetic testing for inherited mutations using comprehensive gene panels for hereditary cancer syndromes (e.g., ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CDKN2A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2, STK11, TP53) recommended for any patient with confirmed ampullary adenocarcinoma or positive family history. Genetic counseling recommended for positive results.

Multidisciplinary consultation involving diagnostic imaging, interventional endoscopy, medical oncology, radiation oncology, surgery, pathology, geriatric medicine, genetic counseling, and palliative care.
Consider diagnostic staging laparoscopy to rule out metastases not detected on imaging prior to surgery or chemoradiation.

surgery.role: Surgical resection is the primary curative treatment for localized ampullary adenocarcinoma. The goal is to achieve an R0 resection, which is associated with improved long-term survival. Surgery also provides tissue for pathologic staging and identification of prognostic factors [AMP-C, MS-6, MS-7].
principles: Decisions about surgical management should involve multidisciplinary consultation at a high-volume center performing at least 15–20 pancreatic resections annually.
Careful intraoperative staging should rule out peritoneal, liver, and distant lymph node metastases; resection should only be done in the absence of distant disease.
Surgery should be efficient, minimizing blood loss, operative time, and cost.
Management of a soft pancreatic remnant should be anticipated.
Achievement of margin-negative resection requires meticulous perivascular dissection and potential vascular resection/reconstruction.
Frozen section analysis of pancreatic neck and bile duct margins is recommended to ensure at least 5 mm clearance.

procedures.name: Pancreatoduodenectomy (Whipple technique)
procedures.approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic; open is traditional, but minimally invasive approaches are increasingly used with potentially quicker recovery and shorter hospital stays.
procedures.indications: Standard procedure for localized ampullary adenocarcinoma, especially for tumors with invasion beyond the sphincter of Oddi or into the pancreas.
procedures.key_complications: Morbidity rates 27%–59%, mortality rates 2%–10%; complications include pancreatic fistula, delayed gastric emptying, hemorrhage, infection.
procedures.operative_mortality: Reported mortality 2%–10% in various studies [AMP-C, MS-7].

procedures.name: Surgical ampullectomy
procedures.approach: Open or laparoscopic; less extensive than pancreatoduodenectomy.
procedures.indications: Selected cases with early-stage ampullary adenocarcinoma, but criteria are not well established; may be associated with higher recurrence rates.
procedures.key_complications: Lower morbidity and mortality compared to pancreatoduodenectomy, but higher recurrence risk.
procedures.operative_mortality: Low, but data limited due to small numbers in studies [MS-8].

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is determined by multidisciplinary consensus based on high-quality imaging (pancreatic protocol CT or MRI). Tumors are considered resectable if there is no evidence of distant metastases and no encasement of major vessels (celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery) that precludes safe resection. locally advanced disease may be treated with systemic therapy or referred for second opinion [AMP-4, AMP-A, MS-6].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Biliary stent placement is not routinely recommended prior to planned surgery; however, a stent may be considered for symptoms of cholangitis/fever, severe symptomatic jaundice (intense pruritus), or if surgery is delayed for neoadjuvant therapy. ERCP-guided biliary drainage is preferred. Stents should be as short as feasible. Self-expanding metal stents (SEMS) should only be placed if tissue diagnosis is confirmed. For neoadjuvant therapy, fully covered SEMS are preferred since they can be removed/exchanged [AMP-B, MS-6].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) plays a role in localized, locally advanced, recurrent, and palliative settings for ampullary adenocarcinoma. The goal is to sterilize vessel margins, enhance the likelihood of margin-negative resection, provide local control to prevent or delay progression or recurrence, and palliate symptoms such as pain, bleeding, or obstruction [AMP-F, MS-2, MS-12].
principles: Prior to RT initiation, staging should be optimally determined with CT abdomen with contrast and/or MRI with and without contrast.
RT recommendations are based on four clinical scenarios: localized disease (neoadjuvant/adjuvant), locally advanced disease, recurrent disease, and palliative care.
IMRT is preferred over 3D-CRT for conventional or hypofractionated RT, especially if dose escalation is considered.
Motion management strategies (e.g., 4D-CT, respiratory gating) should be considered.
Normal tissue dose constraints must be respected to minimize exposure to organs at risk (OARs) such as duodenum, stomach, liver, kidneys, spinal cord, and bowel.

dose_frameworks.name: Conventional fractionation for chemoradiation
dose_frameworks.schedule: Daily fractions, typically 5 days per week
dose_frameworks.indication: Localized/locally advanced disease, adjuvant setting, or palliative intent

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT after resection
dose_frameworks.indication: Adjuvant therapy for high-risk features (≥T3, positive nodes, positive margins, poor differentiation, perineural/perivascular invasion)

approaches.name: Neoadjuvant/adjuvant chemoradiation
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Capecitabine or fluorouracil with concurrent RT for all histologic types; gemcitabine with concurrent RT for pancreatobiliary type only.
approaches.indication: Localized disease with high-risk features or adjuvant setting after resection.
approaches.key_trial: Data extrapolated from pancreatic cancer trials; no ampullary-specific phase III data.
approaches.toxicities: Acute gastrointestinal toxicities (nausea, diarrhea), fatigue; late toxicities depend on dose and volumes.

approaches.name: Definitive chemoradiation for locally advanced disease
approaches.indication: Locally advanced unresectable disease without metastases.
approaches.key_trial: Limited data; recommendations based on pancreatic cancer guidelines.

approaches.name: Palliative radiation therapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: Various short courses (e.g., 8 Gy in 1 fraction, 30 Gy in 10 fractions) based on symptom burden and life expectancy.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: May be used if not previously given as part of primary therapy.
approaches.indication: Metastatic disease causing pain, obstruction, or bleeding; non-metastatic disease in patients unfit for definitive therapy.
approaches.key_trial: No specific trials; individualized based on clinical scenario.

approaches.name: Stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Variable (e.g., 33 Gy in 5 fractions); no definitive dose constraints.
approaches.indication: Recurrent disease or oligometastatic disease; should be used at experienced centers or in clinical trials. Contraindicated if direct invasion of bowel or stomach on imaging.
approaches.key_trial: Emerging data; not standard for ampullary cancer.
approaches.toxicities: Risk of toxicity to adjacent organs; requires careful planning.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is utilized across all stages of ampullary adenocarcinoma, including neoadjuvant, adjuvant, and metastatic settings. Treatment selection depends on histologic subtype (pancreatobiliary/mixed vs. intestinal), performance status, and molecular profiling. Most recommendations are extrapolated from pancreatic, biliary tract, and colorectal cancer guidelines due to limited ampullary-specific data [AMP-E, MS-2, MS-6].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Varies by histologic subtype. For pancreatobiliary/mixed type: albumin-bound paclitaxel/gemcitabine, capecitabine/gemcitabine, cisplatin/gemcitabine, or FOLFIRINOX/modified FOLFIRINOX. For intestinal type: CAPEOX, FOLFIRINOX, or FOLFOX. Chemoradiation may follow based on multidisciplinary review.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Limited evidence; intended for high-risk features to downstage or improve resectability [AMP-E, MS-9].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None specified; histologic subtype guides regimen choice.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: For pancreatobiliary/mixed type: fluorouracil/leucovorin (category 1) or gemcitabine (category 1). For intestinal type: fluorouracil/leucovorin (category 1), capecitabine, or FOLFOX/CAPEOX. All types may consider subsequent chemoradiation for high-risk features.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on ESPAC-3 trial showing improved DFS and OS with adjuvant chemotherapy vs. observation [AMP-E, MS-10].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine/gemcitabine, cisplatin/gemcitabine, modified FOLFIRINOX for pancreatobiliary/mixed type; based on extrapolated data.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for metastatic disease, good/intermediate PS
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: For pancreatobiliary/mixed type: albumin-bound paclitaxel/gemcitabine, capecitabine/gemcitabine, cisplatin/gemcitabine, cisplatin/gemcitabine + durvalumab, FOLFIRINOX/modified FOLFIRINOX, FOLFOX, or NALIRIFOX. For intestinal type: CAPEOX, CAPEOX + bevacizumab, FOLFIRI, FOLFIRI + bevacizumab, FOLFIRINOX, FOLFIRINOX + bevacizumab, FOLFOX, or FOLFOX + bevacizumab.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on trials in pancreatic and colorectal cancers; NALIRIFOX has high-level evidence but no advantage over FOLFIRINOX [AMP-E, MS-12, MS-13].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Targeted therapies based on molecular profiling.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Molecular profiling recommended for actionable alterations (e.g., NTRK fusion, MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, RET fusion, BRAF V600E).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for metastatic disease, poor PS
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Palliative and best supportive care preferred. If systemic therapy considered: for pancreatobiliary/mixed type, capecitabine, fluorouracil/leucovorin, or gemcitabine; for intestinal type, capecitabine or fluorouracil/leucovorin.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Focus on minimizing toxicity while maintaining quality of life [AMP-E, MS-12].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Therapy for disease progression, good/intermediate PS
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Clinical trial preferred. For pancreatobiliary/mixed type: if prior gemcitabine-based therapy, use fluoropyrimidine-based regimen (e.g., capecitabine, CAPEOX, fluorouracil/leucovorin, FOLFIRI, FOLFIRINOX, FOLFOX, OFF); if prior fluoropyrimidine-based, use gemcitabine-based regimen (e.g., albumin-bound paclitaxel/gemcitabine, capecitabine/gemcitabine, gemcitabine). For intestinal type: if prior oxaliplatin-based, use FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on sequencing principles from pancreatic and colorectal cancers [AMP-E, MS-14, MS-15].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Targeted therapies based on molecular profiling.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Molecular profiling recommended for actionable alterations.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Therapy for disease progression, poor PS
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Palliative and best supportive care. If systemic therapy considered: for pancreatobiliary/mixed type, gemcitabine, capecitabine, or fluorouracil/leucovorin; for intestinal type, capecitabine or fluorouracil/leucovorin.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Simplified regimens to reduce toxicity [AMP-E, MS-15].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Surveillance and Response Assessment for Ampullary Adenocarcinoma
treatment_response_assessment.timing: After adjuvant therapy, surveillance is recommended every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for up to 5 years as clinically indicated.
response_logic: Serial imaging as indicated to assess disease response, especially in metastatic or recurrent settings.
Response assessment is based on clinical symptoms, imaging findings, and tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9).
For patients on systemic therapy, imaging is typically performed every 2–3 cycles or as clinically indicated.
In metastatic disease, restaging after first-line therapy may guide consideration of local therapies for oligometastatic disease if response or stable disease is achieved.
imaging_recommendations: Chest CT and CT or MRI of abdomen and pelvis with contrast.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy of new lesions may be considered to confirm progression or histologic transformation.
For local recurrence, salvage options include resection if feasible, radiation therapy, or systemic therapy.
Clinical trial enrollment is encouraged for progressive disease.

prognosis.overall: Ampullary adenocarcinoma is a rare malignancy, accounting for 0.2% of gastrointestinal malignancies and 6% of all periampullary cancers [2]. The 5-year overall survival (OS) for ampullary cancer is between 35% and 50%, but prognosis varies based on factors such as age, TNM classification, differentiation, grade, and treatment modality [3-9]. Ampullary tumors generally have a more favorable outcome compared to other periampullary malignancies, with a median survival of 47 months versus 19, 23, and 54 months for pancreatic, biliary, and duodenal cancer, respectively [10].
prognostic_factors: TNM stage, differentiation, grade, and treatment modality [3-9]
Histologic subtype: pancreatobiliary subtype has higher lymph node involvement and worse survival than intestinal subtype [13,18,20,24-26]
Lymph node metastasis: 5-year disease-specific survival is significantly higher for node-negative versus node-positive disease (59.4% vs. 28.4%; P<.001) [79]
Number of positive lymph nodes: cumulative 5-year survival rates were 85% with 0 positive nodes, 63% with 1-3, and 0% with ≥4 (P<.0001) [89]
Perineural invasion: median survival 18.7 vs. 51.9 months for cancers with vs. without (P=.001) [14]
Tumor size, depth of invasion, lymphovascular invasion, resection margin status [4-6,8,12,66,76,80-83]
Tumor markers CEA and CA 19-9, though prognostic utility is controversial [8,12,81,82,86]
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis is influenced by the ability to achieve an R0 resection, with margin-positive specimens associated with poor long-term survival [4,5]. Early detection may contribute to the relatively favorable prognosis compared to other periampullary cancers [10].

disease_related.management: Endoscopic biliary metal stent (preferred), percutaneous biliary drainage, open biliary-enteric bypass [AMP-G]
For good PS: gastrojejunostomy ± G/J-tube or enteral stent; for poor PS: venting PEG tube or enteral stent [AMP-G]
Low-molecular-weight heparin preferred over warfarin; consider direct oral anticoagulants for patients without luminal tumors [AMP-G]

disease_related.complication: Bleeding from the primary tumor site
disease_related.management: Therapeutic endoscopy, radiation therapy, angiography with embolization [AMP-G]
Early referral to pain specialist; opioids with or without neurolysis; EUS-guided celiac plexus neurolysis; palliative RT; SBRT; high-intensity focused ultrasound; intrathecal drug delivery [AMP-G]
Formal Palliative Medicine Service evaluation; nutritional evaluation with a registered dietitian; pancreatic enzyme replacement for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency [AMP-G]

chemotherapy: Toxicities vary by regimen; modified FOLFIRINOX is often modified by omitting bolus fluorouracil due to high toxicity [AMP-E footnote e]
Nausea, vomiting, myelosuppression, mucositis, hand-foot syndrome (capecitabine), neuropathy (oxaliplatin)
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events as per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [AMP-E footnote j]
targeted_therapy: Specific toxicities based on agent: e.g., dabrafenib/trametinib (fever, rash), entrectinib (dizziness, dysgeusia)
surgery: Postoperative complications: pancreatic fistula, delayed gastric emptying, hemorrhage, infection
radiation: Acute gastrointestinal toxicity (diarrhea, nausea), potential damage to organs at risk (duodenum, stomach, liver, kidneys, spinal cord) [AMP-F]

supportive_care.overview: Principles of Palliation and Supportive Care (AMP-G) aim to prevent and amelater suffering while ensuring optimal quality of life for patients with ampullary cancer.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Nutritional evaluation with a registered dietitian when available; pancreatic enzyme replacement for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (starting dose at least 48,000 units lipase with meals, preferably 72,000) [AMP-G].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not explicitly detailed in the provided text; standard antiemetic guidelines apply based on chemotherapy regimen used.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not explicitly mentioned in the provided text.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Low-molecular-weight heparin preferred over warfarin for thromboembolic disease prophylaxis/treatment; consider direct oral anticoagulants for patients without luminal tumors [AMP-G].
supportive_care.pain_management: Early referral to pain or palliative care specialist; opioids with or without neurolysis; EUS-guided celiac plexus neurolysis (fluoroscopic- or CT-guided if unavailable); celiac plexus radiation/radiosurgery; SBRT; high-intensity focused ultrasound; consider palliative radiation with or without chemotherapy if not already given; intrathecal drug delivery for severe tumor-associated abdominal pain unresponsive to optimal analgesic administration [AMP-G].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Formal Palliative Medicine Service evaluation when available; consider encouraging advanced care planning [AMP-G].
supportive_care.dental_care: Not mentioned in the provided source text.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After adjuvant therapy, surveillance is recommended every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months for up to 5 years or as clinically indicated [AMP-5].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: To detect recurrence early, as recurrences can occur as early as <6 months and as late as 22.5 months after surgery [5,12,81,84,88,100].
follow_up.late_effects_screening: Not explicitly detailed in the provided text; long-term follow-up should consider potential delayed treatment effects such as pancreatic exocrine/endocrine insufficiency after surgery or radiation-induced toxicity.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence rates range between 28% and 55%, with distant recurrences making up ~80% in many studies [5,81,84,88,100]. The most common site of distant metastasis is the liver (38%–65% of distant recurrences); other sites include peritoneum, lung, and bones [5,81,84,88,100]. Pancreatic invasion and tumor size are predictive of locoregional recurrence, while lymph node involvement is the sole predictor for liver metastasis [5].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: History and physical (H&P) examination every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months for up to 5 years as clinically indicated [AMP-5]
imaging_strategy: Chest CT and CT or MRI of abdomen and pelvis with contrast at each follow-up [AMP-5]
Dedicated pancreatic protocol CT (preferred) or MRI with and without contrast for initial staging and follow-up [AMP-A]
Consider MRI for indeterminate liver lesions as clinically indicated [AMP-3]
laboratory_monitoring: CEA and/or CA 19-9 at each follow-up [AMP-5]

special_situations.key_message: Select patients with oligometastatic disease and response/stable disease to systemic therapy may be candidates for local-directed therapy.
special_situations.management: Systemic therapy followed by surgery or other locally directed therapies to liver or lung metastases after multidisciplinary tumor board review [AMP-6].
special_situations.evidence: Limited data; extrapolated from oligometastatic cancer management principles.

special_situations.key_message: Locally advanced disease is treated similar to metastatic disease.
special_situations.management: Systemic therapy with or without subsequent radiation therapy for palliative indications [AMP-6]. Refer to tertiary cancer center for second opinion about unresectability [AMP-4 footnote o].

special_situations.key_message: Poor PS patients should receive palliative and best supportive care.
special_situations.management: Palliative and best supportive care; consider systemic therapy or targeted therapy based on molecular profiling if clinically indicated; palliative RT [AMP-6, AMP-7].

special_situations.key_message: Immunotherapy is a treatment option for MSI-H/dMMR tumors.
special_situations.management: Pembrolizumab (if MSI-H, dMMR, or TMB-H ≥10 mut/Mb); ipilimumab + nivolumab (if MSI-H or dMMR, for intestinal type only); nivolumab monotherapy for poor PS [AMP-E 3 of 11, AMP-E 5 of 11].

special_situations.key_message: Targeted therapy with TRK inhibitors is an option.
special_situations.management: Entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib [AMP-E 3 of 11, AMP-E 5 of 11].
special_situations.evidence: Basket trials: larotrectinib ORR 75% [31]; entrectinib ORR 57% [29,30]; repotrectinib FDA approval [32].

special_situations.key_message: HER2-targeted therapy may be considered for HER2-positive (IHC3+ or IHC2+ with FISH-amplified) tumors.
special_situations.management: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki for good PS [AMP-E 5 of 11].
special_situations.evidence: DESTINY-PanTumor02 trial: ORR 45.3% in HER2-expressing solid tumors [53]; DESTINY-CRC01 trial in colorectal cancer [181].
Phase II trial in BRCA/PALB2-mutated pancreatic cancer: ORR 74.1%, median OS 15.5 months [177].

clinical_pearls: Ampullary cancer is rare (0.2% of GI malignancies) but important due to pathologic variations and prognosis [2].
Histologic subtyping (pancreatobiliary vs. intestinal) is crucial for prognosis and may guide therapy selection; pancreatobiliary subtype has worse survival [13,18,20,24-26].
Genetic testing for inherited mutations (ATM, BRCA1/2, CDKN2A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2, STK11, TP53) is recommended for all patients with ampullary adenocarcinoma [AMP-3 footnote b].
Neoadjuvant therapy can be considered in patients with high-risk features (equivocal imaging, markedly elevated CA 19-9/CEA, large tumors, etc.) [AMP-4].
Adjuvant chemotherapy (5-FU/leucovorin or gemcitabine) improves survival compared to observation (ESPAC-3 trial) [5].
Molecular profiling is recommended for metastatic disease to identify actionable alterations (fusions, mutations, amplifications, MSI, dMMR, TMB) [AMP-3 footnote h].
Endoscopic ampullectomy is an option for benign ampullary adenomas, but should be performed at high-volume centers; surgery is preferred for invasive cancer [AMP-2].
Chemoradiation may be used in the adjuvant setting for high-risk features (e.g., positive margins, node-positive disease) [AMP-5].

key_trials.population: Patients with resected periampullary adenocarcinoma (including ampullary cancer)
key_trials.intervention: Adjuvant chemotherapy with fluorouracil/leucovorin or gemcitabine
key_trials.key_result: No significant difference in OS between fluorouracil/leucovorin vs. gemcitabine (median OS 23.0 vs. 23.6 months). However, chemotherapy vs. observation improved OS (HR 0.75; 95% CI 0.57-0.98; P=.03) [5].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Treatment-related serious adverse events were higher with fluorouracil/leucovorin than gemcitabine (49% vs. 30%; P=.002) [5].
key_trials.practice_change: Established adjuvant chemotherapy (5-FU/leucovorin or gemcitabine) as standard of care for resected periampullary cancers.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab in Patients With Noncolorectal High Microsatellite Instability/Mismatch Repair-Deficient Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with advanced solid tumors including MSI-H/dMMR cancers (27 tumor types)
key_trials.key_result: ORR 34.3%, median PFS 4.1 months, median OS 23.5 months [34,35].
key_trials.practice_change: Supported pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors regardless of histology.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab in DNA Mismatch Repair-Deficient/Microsatellite Instability-High Metastatic Colorectal Cancer
key_trials.population: Previously treated MSI-H/dMMR metastatic colorectal cancer
key_trials.practice_change: Supported nivolumab plus ipilimumab for MSI-H/dMMR cancers.

key_trials.full_name: Nanoliposomal Irinotecan with Fluorouracil and Folinic Acid in Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer After Previous Gemcitabine-Based Therapy
key_trials.population: Patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer previously treated with gemcitabine-based therapy
key_trials.practice_change: Established nanoliposomal irinotecan + fluorouracil/folinic acid as a second-line option for metastatic pancreatic cancer.

key_trials.full_name: FOLFIRINOX or Gemcitabine as Adjuvant Therapy for Pancreatic Cancer
key_trials.key_result: mFOLFIRINOX significantly improved DFS (median 21.6 vs. 12.8 months; HR 0.58; 95% CI 0.46-0.74; P<.001) and OS (median 54.4 vs. 35.0 months; HR 0.64; 95% CI 0.48-0.86; P=.003) [137].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-4 adverse events higher with mFOLFIRINOX (75.9% vs. 52.9%) [137].
key_trials.practice_change: Established mFOLFIRINOX as a standard adjuvant therapy for resected pancreatic cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in Patients With HER2-Expressing Solid Tumors
key_trials.population: Patients with advanced solid tumors including pancreatic cancer
key_trials.key_result: ORR 45.3% across all tumor types; in 25 patients with pancreatic cancer, ORR not separately reported [53,179].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median OS and PFS not separately reported for ampullary cancer [53,179].
key_trials.practice_change: Supported trastuzumab deruxtecan for HER2-expressing solid tumors, including potential use in ampullary cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Chemotherapy with Gemcitabine and Long-Term Outcomes Among Patients with Resected Pancreatic Cancer
key_trials.key_result: Gemcitabine significantly improved DFS (median 13.4 vs. 6.9 months; HR 0.55; 95% CI 0.43-0.71; P<.001) and OS (median 22.8 vs. 20.2 months; HR 0.76; 95% CI 0.57-0.99; P=.047) [127].
key_trials.practice_change: Established gemcitabine as standard adjuvant therapy for resected pancreatic cancer.
